steps.prompt: Which word is the main imperative verb?

hints.sint: 'sint' is subjunctive in the 'cum' clause — not the main verb
hints.coepisti: 'coepisti' is perfect indicative in the relative clause — not the main verb
hints.Quae: 'Quae' is a relative pronoun meaning 'which things'

hints.to drive through: 'to drive through' is closer to 'peragere' — 'pergere' means 'to continue/go on'

hints.Indicative: Indicative would state a fact — 'perge' is a command (imperative singular)
hints.Subjunctive: Subjunctive would be 'pergas' — 'perge' is a direct imperative
hints.Infinitive: Infinitive would be 'pergere' — 'perge' is the imperative

hints.perdo, perdere, perdidi, perditum: These are the parts of 'perdere' (to destroy)
hints.peto, petere, petivi, petitum: These are the parts of 'petere' (to seek/attack)
hints.pango, pangere, pepigi, pactum: These are the parts of 'pangere' (to fix/agree)

hints.Present indicative: Present indicative would be 'sunt' — 'sint' is subjunctive after 'cum'
hints.Imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'essent' — 'sint' is present subjunctive
hints.Perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'fuerint' — 'sint' is present

steps.prompt: Why is the subjunctive used with 'cum … sint'?
steps.target: Cum causal/concessive — 'since these things are so'
choices: Cum causal/concessive — 'since these things are so'

hints.Cum temporal — 'when': Temporal 'cum' can take subjunctive, but 'quae cum ita sint' is a standard causal formula meaning 'since'
hints.Purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut/ne' — 'cum' here is causal
hints.Indirect question: Indirect questions use interrogative words — 'cum' is a conjunction meaning 'since'

hints.Present indicative: 'coepi' is a defective verb with only perfect-system forms — 'coepisti' is perfect
hints.Imperfect indicative: There is no imperfect of 'coepi' — it is a defective verb used only in perfect tenses

steps.prompt: 'Quae cum ita sint' is a common Ciceronian transition. What does it mean?
steps.target: Since these things are so / under these circumstances
choices: Since these things are so / under these circumstances

hints.Whatever happens: 'Whatever happens' would be 'quicquid accidat' — 'quae cum ita sint' summarizes preceding arguments
hints.Although these things exist: 'Although' would use 'quamquam' or 'cum' + concessive — this is causal 'since'

translation: Since these things are so, Catiline, continue where you began.
